closed_children.title: state_remote config field + state-branch sync/discovery plumbing
bl init safety: adopt a configured state_remote; never clobber a shared branch
bl remaster: re-point the task state branch, with reconcile and --detach

description:
balls already owns a distributed task-sync mechanism: the balls/tasks orphan ref, negotiated through the git-remote Participant (the one negotiation primitive in claim_sync.rs / SPEC-lifecycle-sync-participants.md). Multiple clones of one repo already share task state because they all negotiate the same balls/tasks against a common origin. The only thing hardwired is the assumption that the state-branch remote IS the code remote: store discovery resolves the store from the code repo's git_common_dir (store.rs:60) and state-branch setup tracks origin/balls/tasks (store_init.rs).

A project that spans multiple repos needs nothing more than to break that one assumption. Introduce a single config field, state_remote (default: origin), naming the git remote/URL whose balls/tasks ref is authoritative for this repo. A client repo points state_remote at a shared task hub: a tiny code-less repo whose only content is the balls/tasks orphan branch (or the "main" code repo, which is just the special case where the hub also carries code). Every participating repo then negotiates the SAME balls/tasks through the EXISTING negotiation primitive.

WHY THIS SHAPE (not a client/server protocol): the load-bearing invariants are "git is the database; no sidecar store; bl is a convenience wrapper over publishable git commands" (SPEC-orphan-branch-state.md S2) and "one negotiation primitive; no parallel sync subsystems" (SPEC-lifecycle-sync-participants.md S2). A client->main RPC relationship would violate both: a second sync path, and the main repo as a privileged gateway. Decoupling state_remote from the code remote adds zero new machinery: same orphan ref, same git-remote Participant, same fetch/non-FF/retry loop, pointed elsewhere. It works offline, needs no daemon, and the "main repo" has no availability requirement beyond git fetch (identical to today's origin). Recommended topology for a true multi-repo project is a dedicated code-less hub repo; "main code repo hosts it" is the degenerate case of the same design.

ID COLLISIONS: a single shared balls/tasks makes the existing per-store guard sufficient. store.task_exists + the 1ms-bump retry (id_gen.rs:20) already enforce uniqueness within the shared store, provided init/remaster fetch the shared branch before the first id is generated. The only residual window (two repos creating tasks fully offline then both pushing) is exactly the non-FF case the negotiation primitive already reconciles on push. No id-schema change; no per-repo prefixes (prefixes would break the content-hash stable-id design).

BACKWARDS COMPAT STRATEGY: state_remote defaults to origin, byte-identical to today; an unmodified .balls/config.json produces the exact same fetch/push targets, commits, and exit codes. The field lives in the committed .balls/config.json so the project link travels with the codebase, honoring the existing precedence (committed < .balls/local/config.json < per-invocation flag; SPEC-lifecycle S11) so a fork can detach with a local override without touching shared config. Additive; ignored by older bl (no deny_unknown_fields on Config).

ACCEPTED CAVEAT (per user 2026-05-17): an old bl in a client repo ignores state_remote and fetches/pushes balls/tasks against origin (the code remote) instead of the hub, silently operating on a local/empty state branch. Detectable via an advisory min_bl_version in config, but not engineered against: documented, not prevented. Non-destructive: the old client cannot corrupt a hub it never contacts, and bl remaster reconciles its local-only tasks on upgrade.
title: state_remote: one project, many repos via a shared task branch
